examples.explanation:
Start at node 0 (path=[0], dist=0, visited={0}).
Option 1: Go to node 1 (path=[0,1], dist=2, visited={0,1}).
From node 1: Go to node 2 (path=[0,1,2], dist=3, visited={0,1,2}).
From node 2: Go to node 3 (path=[0,1,2,3], dist=6, visited={0,1,2,3}) → reached dest, update best_dist=6, best_path=[0,1,2,3].
Backtrack from node 3 to node 2 (path=[0,1,2], visited={0,1,2}).
From node 2: No more neighbors, backtrack to node 1 (path=[0,1], visited={0,1}).
From node 1: Go to node 3 (path=[0,1,3], dist=9, visited={0,1,3}) → update best_dist=6 (unchanged).
Backtrack to node 1, then node 0 (path=[0], visited={0}).
Option 2: Go to node 2 (path=[0,2], dist=4, visited={0,2}).
From node 2: Go to node 3 (path=[0,2,3], dist=7, visited={0,2,3}) → update best_dist=6 (unchanged).
Backtrack, finish. Best: dist=6, path=[0,1,2,3].

Start at node 0 (path=[0], dist=0).
Go to node 1 (path=[0,1], dist=1).
Go to node 2 (path=[0,1,2], dist=2).
Go to node 3 (path=[0,1,2,3], dist=3) → reached dest, update best_dist=3, best_path=[0,1,2,3].
Backtrack, no alternatives. Result: dist=3, path=[0,1,2,3].

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(V!)
approaches.summary: Explore all possible paths from source to destination using depth-first search, tracking the shortest distance and path. This is impractical for all but the smallest graphs due to exponential time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force DFS for Shortest Path with Path Reconstruction
ladders.desc: Complete implementation exploring all possible paths from source to destination using DFS, tracking the shortest distance and reconstructing the optimal path.

explanation.prose: This approach solves the shortest path problem by exhaustively exploring every possible route from source to destination in a graph. The intuition is simple: if we check every possible path, we're guaranteed to find the shortest one. We use depth-first search to systematically traverse all paths, maintaining a visited set to avoid cycles. At each step, we add the current node to the path and accumulate the distance. When we reach the destination, we compare the current path's distance with the best found so far, updating if better. The key insight is that we must backtrack properly—removing nodes from the path and visited set—to explore alternative routes. This brute force method has factorial time complexity O(V!) because in the worst case (complete graph), we can visit nodes in any order, leading to V! possible permutations.

connectsTo.reason: This full solution integrates the DFS traversal (L1) with distance tracking and path reconstruction logic

spaceComplexity.note: for recursion stack and visited tracking

commonMistakes: forgetting to backtrack (causing infinite loops), not handling disconnected graphs, and inefficiently storing paths (should use lists with O(
explanation.codePattern: Use recursion with parameters for current node, visited set, current path, current distance, and references to best distance/path

From node 2: Go to node 3 (path=[0,1,2,3], dist=6, visited={0,1,2,3}) → reached dest, update best_dist=6, best_path=[0,1,2,3].
Backtrack from node 3 to node 2 (path=[0,1,2], visited={0,1,2}).
From node 2: No more neighbors, backtrack to node 1 (path=[0,1], visited={0,1}).
From node 1: Go to node 3 (path=[0,1,3], dist=9, visited={0,1,3}) → update best_dist=6 (unchanged).
Backtrack to node 1, then node 0 (path=[0], visited={0}).
Option 2: Go to node 2 (path=[0,2], dist=4, visited={0,2}).
From node 2: Go to node 3 (path=[0,2,3], dist=7, visited={0,2,3}) → update best_dist=6 (unchanged).
Backtrack, finish. Best: dist=6, path=[0,1,2,3].

Go to node 1 (path=[0,1], dist=1).
Go to node 2 (path=[0,1,2], dist=2).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal with Visited Tracking
ladders.desc: Depth-first search that explores all reachable nodes from a starting point while avoiding cycles.

explanation.prose: This sub-routine implements DFS to traverse a graph completely from a given source node. The intuition is to explore as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking, similar to navigating a maze by always taking the first available turn. We use recursion (or a stack) to manage the traversal order. The algorithm starts at the source node, marks it as visited, and recursively visits all unvisited neighbors. The visited set prevents revisiting nodes, which avoids infinite loops in cyclic graphs.

connectsTo.reason: This DFS forms the backbone of the brute force approach—instead of just traversing, L0 adds distance accumulation and destination checking

timeComplexity.note: where V is vertices and E is edges, as each node and edge is processed once

spaceComplexity.note: for the recursion stack and visited set

commonMistakes: not marking nodes as visited before recursion (causing cycles)
using a list for visited (slow lookups)
explanation.codePattern: Recursive function dfs(node, visited) that iterates through graph[node] and calls dfs(neighbor, visited) for unvisited neighbors

examples.trace:
Start at node 0, visited={0}.
Visit neighbor 1 (unvisited), go to node 1, visited={0,1}.
From node 1, visit neighbor 2 (unvisited), go to node 2, visited={0,1,2}.
From node 2, no unvisited neighbors, backtrack to node 1.
Node 1 has no more unvisited neighbors, backtrack to node 0.
From node 0, neighbor 2 already visited, finish. Order: 0→1→2.

Start at node 0, visited={0}.
Visit neighbor 1 (unvisited), go to node 1, visited={0,1}.
From node 1, neighbor 0 already visited, backtrack.
Finish. Order: 0→1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backtracking for Path Exploration
ladders.desc: Systematically building and dismantling paths to explore all possible routes in a graph.

explanation.prose: This core logic involves backtracking, a technique where we incrementally build a solution (a path) and undo choices to explore alternatives. The intuition is like trying every combination in a lock: we add a number (node), check if it works (reaches destination), then remove it to try the next number. In graph terms, we start with an empty path, add nodes as we traverse edges, and when we hit a dead end or finish exploring a branch, we remove the last node (backtrack) to try other neighbors. The key insight is that backtracking allows us to reuse the same path list efficiently—we append when moving forward, pop when backtracking. Time complexity depends on graph structure; in worst case, we explore all permutations of nodes.

connectsTo.reason: Backtracking extends DFS by maintaining a mutable path that changes during traversal, whereas basic DFS just tracks visited nodes

commonMistakes: not popping nodes after recursion (corrupting the path)

examples.input: Graph: {0: {1: 1, 2: 1}, 1: {3: 1}, 2: {3: 1}, 3: {}}, start=0, explore all paths to 3
examples.trace:
Start: path=[].
Add node 0: path=[0].
Try neighbor 1: add node 1, path=[0,1].
From node 1, try neighbor 3: add node 3, path=[0,1,3] → record path.
Backtrack: pop 3, path=[0,1]; pop 1, path=[0].
Try neighbor 2: add node 2, path=[0,2].
From node 2, try neighbor 3: add node 3, path=[0,2,3] → record path.
Backtrack: pop 3, path=[0,2]; pop 2, path=[0]; pop 0, path=[].

examples.input: Graph: {0: {1: 1}, 1: {2: 1}, 2: {}}, start=0, explore all paths to 2
examples.trace:
Start: path=[].
Add node 0: path=[0].
Try neighbor 1: add node 1, path=[0,1].
From node 1, try neighbor 2: add node 2, path=[0,1,2] → record path.
Backtrack: pop 2, path=[0,1]; pop 1, path=[0]; pop 0, path=[].

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Representing a graph using a dictionary where keys are nodes and values are dictionaries of neighbors with edge weights.

explanation.prose: This building block involves representing a graph in code, specifically using an adjacency list. The intuition is that a graph is a collection of nodes (vertices) connected by edges; an adjacency list efficiently stores each node's immediate connections. In this representation, we use a dictionary: keys are node identifiers (e.g., integers), and values are dictionaries mapping neighbor nodes to edge weights. For example, graph[0] = {1: 5, 2: 3} means node 0 has edges to node 1 with weight 5 and node 2 with weight 3. This representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs (O(V + E)) and allows quick access to a node's neighbors. Time complexity for looking up neighbors is O(1) on average.

connectsTo.reason: Backtracking operates directly on this adjacency list to traverse edges

commonMistakes: using lists for neighbors without weights (inadequate for weighted graphs)
assuming undirected graphs when they're directed

examples.trace:
Start with empty dict: {}.
Add edge (0,1,5): graph[0] = {1:5}, graph[1] = {}.
Add edge (0,2,3): graph[0] becomes {1:5, 2:3}, graph[2] = {}.
Add edge (1,2,1): graph[1] becomes {2:1}.
Final: {0: {1:5, 2:3}, 1: {2:1}, 2: {}}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Dictionary Keys and Values
ladders.desc: Accessing and looping over elements in a dictionary, a fundamental data structure operation.

explanation.prose: This basic operation involves working with dictionaries in Python, specifically iterating through keys and values. The intuition is that a dictionary stores key-value pairs, and we often need to process all elements—for example, to visit all neighbors of a node in a graph. The algorithm uses a for loop: for key in dict: accesses keys; for value in dict.values(): accesses values; for key, value in dict.items(): accesses both. items() to get neighbors and edge weights.

connectsTo.reason: When traversing a graph represented as an adjacency list, we iterate through graph[node]

commonMistakes: modifying dictionary size during iteration (causing errors)
not handling missing keys (use .get() or checks)

examples.trace:
Initialize dict d = {0:5, 1:3, 2:8}.
Iterate keys: for k in d: print(k) → outputs 0, then 1, then 2.
Iterate values: for v in d.values(): print(v) → outputs 5, then 3, then 8.
Iterate items: for k,v in d.items(): print(k,v) → outputs (0,5), (1,3), (2,8).

d = {'a':1, 'b':2}.
Initialize sum=0.
Iterate values: for v in d.values(): sum += v.
First v=1, sum=1.
Second v=2, sum=3.
Result: 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Variables and Basic Data Types
ladders.desc: Understanding variables as named storage for data, and basic types like integers, lists, and dictionaries.

explanation.prose: This foundation concept covers variables and basic data types, which are essential for any programming. The intuition is that variables are like labeled boxes where we store information—for example, a variable 'distance' might hold a number representing how far we've traveled. Basic types include integers (whole numbers, e.g., 5), lists (ordered collections, e.g., [1,2,3]), and dictionaries (key-value pairs, e.g., {0:1}). We use variables to track state in algorithms: an integer for current distance, a list for the current path, a dictionary for the graph. Time and space analysis isn't applicable here as it's a basic operation.

connectsTo.reason: Iterating through dictionaries requires understanding that dictionaries store data in key-value format

commonMistakes: adding a string to an integer)

Assign x = 5: creates variable x with value 5.
Assign lst = [1,2]: creates list with elements 1 and 2.
Assign d = {0:1}: creates dictionary with key 0 mapping to value 1.
Variables now hold these values.

approaches.name: Dijkstra with Parent Array O((V+E) log V)
approaches.summary: Use Dijkstra's algorithm with a min-heap to find shortest distances, and maintain a parent array to reconstruct the path by backtracking from the destination. This is the standard approach for non-negative weighted graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Dijkstra's Algorithm with Path Reconstruction
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Dijkstra's algorithm using a min-heap to find shortest distances and reconstruct the actual path from source to destination.

explanation.prose: This is the complete solution to finding the shortest path in a weighted graph with non-negative edges using Dijkstra's algorithm. The intuition is that we can systematically explore nodes in order of increasing distance from the source, guaranteeing that when we first visit a node, we've found the shortest path to it. We maintain a min-heap (priority queue) to always process the node with the smallest known distance next. For path reconstruction, we keep a parent array that records the predecessor node for each node in the shortest path tree. When we update a node's distance, we also update its parent to be the current node. After computing all distances, we can reconstruct the path from destination to source by following the parent pointers backward, then reverse it. The algorithm: 1) Initialize distances array with infinity except source=0, parent array with -1, and min-heap with (0, source). 2) While heap not empty: pop node u with smallest distance. 3) For each neighbor v of u: if distance[u] + weight(u,v) < distance[v], update distance[v], set parent[v]=u, push (distance[v], v) to heap. 4) After distances computed, reconstruct path from dest to source using parent array. Time: O((V+E) log V) for heap operations. Space: O(V+E) for adjacency list and auxiliary arrays.

connectsTo.reason: This integrates the parent tracking mechanism with the core Dijkstra algorithm

commonMistakes: Not checking if popped node has already been processed (can use visited set or check if current distance > stored distance)
forgetting to handle unreachable nodes in path reconstruction
explanation.codePattern: Use heapq in Python, maintain distances and parents arrays, reconstruct path by backtracking from destination

examples.input: Graph: 4 nodes (0-3), edges: 0→1 weight=4, 0→2 weight=1, 2→1 weight=2, 1→3 weight=1, 2→3 weight=5. Source=0, Destination=3
examples.trace: Initial: dist=[0,∞,∞,∞], parent=[-1,-1,-1,-1], heap=[(0,0)]. Pop (0,0): u=0, dist[0]=0. Neighbors: v=1 weight4: 0+4=4<∞ → update dist[1]=4, parent[1]=0, push (4,1). v=2 weight1: 0+1=1<∞ → update dist[2]=1, parent[2]=0, push (1,2). Heap: [(1,2),(4,1)]. Pop (1,2): u=2, dist[2]=1. Neighbors: v=1 weight2: 1+2=3<4 → update dist[1]=3, parent[1]=2, push (3,1). v=3 weight5: 1+5=6<∞ → update dist[3]=6, parent[3]=2, push (6,3). Heap: [(3,1),(4,1),(6,3)]. Pop (3,1): u=1, dist[1]=3. Neighbors: v=3 weight1: 3+1=4<6 → update dist[3]=4, parent[3]=1, push (4,3). Heap: [(4,1),(4,3),(6,3)]. Pop (4,1): u=1 already processed (dist[1]=3<4) → skip. Pop (4,3): u=3, dist[3]=4. No unprocessed neighbors. Heap empty. Reconstruct path from dest=3: parent[3]=1, parent[1]=2, parent[2]=0, parent[0]=-1 → path=[3,1,2,0] reversed → [0,2,1,3]. Distance=4.

examples.input: Graph: 3 nodes (0-2), edges: 0→1 weight=5, 1→2 weight=3. Source=0, Destination=2
examples.trace: Initial: dist=[0,∞,∞], parent=[-1,-1,-1], heap=[(0,0)]. Pop (0,0): u=0, dist[0]=0. Neighbors: v=1 weight5: 0+5=5<∞ → update dist[1]=5, parent[1]=0, push (5,1). Heap: [(5,1)]. Pop (5,1): u=1, dist[1]=5. Neighbors: v=2 weight3: 5+3=8<∞ → update dist[2]=8, parent[2]=1, push (8,2). Heap: [(8,2)]. Pop (8,2): u=2, dist[2]=8. No neighbors. Heap empty. Reconstruct path: parent[2]=1, parent[1]=0, parent[0]=-1 → [2,1,0] reversed → [0,1,2]. Distance=8.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Parent Array Backtracking for Path Reconstruction
ladders.desc: Given a parent array and destination node, reconstruct the shortest path from source to destination by following parent pointers backward.

explanation.prose: This sub-routine handles the path reconstruction part of Dijkstra's algorithm. The intuition is that the parent array forms a reverse linked list from each node back to the source along the shortest path. By starting at the destination and repeatedly moving to the parent node, we trace the path backward to the source. We need to handle cases where the destination is unreachable (parent is -1) or when the destination equals the source. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty path list. 2) Set current = destination. 3) While current != -1: append current to path, set current = parent[current]. 4) Reverse the path list (since we collected it backward). 5) If the path is empty or the first element isn't the source (indicating unreachable), return empty path. Time: O(V) in worst case (path length). Space: O(V) for the path list.

connectsTo.reason: This is called after Dijkstra completes to produce the actual path

commonMistakes: not handling unreachable nodes (check if parent[dest]==-1
infinite loop if parent pointers form a cycle (shouldn't happen in Dijkstra)
explanation.codePattern: Use while loop with parent array, build list, reverse it, validate

examples.trace: Start at dest=3. parent[3]=2 → append 3, current=2. parent[2]=1 → append 2, current=1. parent[1]=0 → append 1, current=0. parent[0]=-1 → append 0, current=-1. Loop ends. Path collected: [3,2,1,0]. Reverse: [0,1,2,3]. Validate: first element=0 matches source.

Start at dest=3. parent[3]=1 → append 3, current=1. parent[1]=-1 → append 1, current=-1. Loop ends. Path: [3,1]. Reverse: [1,3] → first element=1 ≠ source=0, but parent[1]=-1 and 1≠0, so dest unreachable? Wait, check: parent[3]=1, parent[1]=-1, but source=0. Actually, node 1 is unreachable from source (parent=-1), so path from 0 to 3 doesn't exist. Should return empty path. Correction: After reverse, path=[1,3], first element=1 ≠ source, so return [].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Dijkstra's Algorithm with Min-Heap for Shortest Distances
ladders.desc: Implement Dijkstra's algorithm using a min-heap to compute shortest distances from a source to all nodes in a weighted graph with non-negative edges.

explanation.prose: This is the core of Dijkstra's algorithm without path reconstruction. The intuition is greedy: always expand the node with the smallest known distance, because in graphs with non-negative weights, once we process a node, we've found the shortest path to it. We maintain a min-heap (priority queue) to efficiently get the next node to process. The algorithm: 1) Initialize distance array with infinity for all nodes except source=0. 2) Push (0, source) to min-heap. 3) While heap not empty: pop node u with smallest distance. If distance[u] is less than current popped distance (meaning we already found a shorter path), skip. 4) For each neighbor v of u: if distance[u] + weight(u,v) < distance[v], update distance[v] and push (distance[v], v) to heap. This ensures we explore nodes in order of increasing distance. Time: O((V+E) log V) because each edge may cause a heap push. Space: O(V+E) for graph and O(V) for heap.

connectsTo.reason: This produces the distance array and implicitly the parent array if we track predecessors

explanation.codePattern: Use heapq in Python, while heap, for neighbors, relax edges

examples.input: Graph: 3 nodes (0-2), edges: 0→1 weight=4, 0→2 weight=1, 2→1 weight=2. Source=0
examples.trace: Initial: dist=[0,∞,∞], heap=[(0,0)]. Pop (0,0): u=0. Neighbors: v=1 weight4: 0+4=4<∞ → update dist[1]=4, push (4,1). v=2 weight1: 0+1=1<∞ → update dist[2]=1, push (1,2). Heap: [(1,2),(4,1)]. Pop (1,2): u=2. Neighbors: v=1 weight2: 1+2=3<4 → update dist[1]=3, push (3,1). Heap: [(3,1),(4,1)]. Pop (3,1): u=1. No unprocessed neighbors. Pop (4,1): u=1 already processed (dist[1]=3<4) → skip. Heap empty. Distances: [0,3,1].

examples.input: Graph: 4 nodes (0-3), edges: 0→1 weight=2, 1→2 weight=3, 2→3 weight=1. Source=0
examples.trace: Initial: dist=[0,∞,∞,∞], heap=[(0,0)]. Pop (0,0): u=0. Neighbors: v=1 weight2: 0+2=2<∞ → update dist[1]=2, push (2,1). Heap: [(2,1)]. Pop (2,1): u=1. Neighbors: v=2 weight3: 2+3=5<∞ → update dist[2]=5, push (5,2). Heap: [(5,2)]. Pop (5,2): u=2. Neighbors: v=3 weight1: 5+1=6<∞ → update dist[3]=6, push (6,3). Heap: [(6,3)]. Pop (6,3): u=3. No neighbors. Heap empty. Distances: [0,2,5,6].

ladders.title: Building Block — Min-Heap (Priority Queue) Operations for Graph Traversal
ladders.desc: Use a min-heap to always process the node with the smallest distance in Dijkstra's algorithm, including push, pop, and decrease-key operations.

explanation.prose: This building block focuses on the heap data structure used in Dijkstra's algorithm. The intuition is that we need to repeatedly get the node with the smallest tentative distance, and a min-heap allows O(log n) for both insertion and extraction of the minimum. In Dijkstra, we push nodes with their distances, pop the minimum, and sometimes push updated distances (simulating decrease-key by pushing duplicate entries). The algorithm: 1) Initialize empty min-heap. 2) Push (distance, node) tuples. 3) While heap not empty: pop smallest (dist, node). 4) If dist > current stored distance for node, skip (this handles duplicates). 5) Otherwise, process node and push updates for neighbors. Time: O(log n) per push/pop. Space: O(n) for heap.

connectsTo.reason: The heap enables the greedy selection in Dijkstra

examples.input: Initial heap empty. Operations: push (5,1), push (3,2), push (7,3), pop, push (4,4), pop, pop
Heap with duplicates: push (10,0), push (5,1), pop, push (3,1), pop, pop
examples.trace: Start: heap=[]. Push (10,0): heap=[(10,0)]. Push (5,1): heap=[(5,1),(10,0)]. Pop: returns (5,1), heap=[(10,0)]. Push (3,1): heap=[(3,1),(10,0)]. Pop: returns (3,1), heap=[(10,0)]. Pop: returns (10,0), heap=[].

Operations: push (7,0), push (3,1), push (5,2), pop, push (1,3), pop
Operations: push (10,0), push (8,1), push (6,2), push (4,3), pop, pop, pop
Operations: push (9,0), push (7,1), push (5,2), pop, push (3,3), pop, pop
Operations: push (2,0), push (2,1), push (2,2), pop, pop, pop
Operations: push (6,0), push (4,1), pop, push (2,2), pop, pop

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Relaxation of Edges in Shortest Path Algorithms
ladders.desc: Update the distance to a neighbor node if a shorter path is found through the current node, a fundamental step in Dijkstra and other shortest path algorithms.

explanation.prose: Relaxation is the core operation where we check if going through the current node u to neighbor v provides a shorter path than currently known. The intuition is that we continuously improve distance estimates until they converge to the shortest paths. Given current node u with distance dist[u], neighbor v with weight w, we compare dist[u] + w with dist[v]. If it's smaller, we update dist[v] to this new value. This is a greedy improvement step. Algorithm: if dist[u] + weight(u,v) < dist[v]: dist[v] = dist[u] + weight(u,v). In Dijkstra, we also update parent[v] = u here. Time: O(1) per edge. Space: O(1).

connectsTo.reason: This operation is performed for each neighbor when a node is popped from the heap

explanation.codePattern: Simple if statement in a loop over neighbors

examples.trace: Calculate dist[u] + weight = 3 + 2 = 5. Compare 5 < 10? Yes. So update dist[v] = 5.
Calculate 7 + 3 = 10. Compare 10 < 5? No. So dist[v] remains 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation Using Adjacency Lists
ladders.desc: Represent a weighted graph as an adjacency list, where each node has a list of its neighbors and corresponding edge weights, the basic structure for graph algorithms.

explanation.prose: This is the foundational concept for storing graphs in algorithms like Dijkstra. An adjacency list represents a graph as an array of lists, where each index corresponds to a node, and the list contains pairs (neighbor, weight) for edges from that node to neighbors. The intuition is that it efficiently stores sparse graphs and allows quick iteration over a node's neighbors. For a directed graph, each edge appears once; for undirected, twice. Example: Graph with nodes 0,1,2 and edges 0→1 weight=4, 0→2 weight=3 would be represented as [[(1,4),(2,3)], [], []]. Algorithm to build: 1) Create list of size V. 2) For each edge (u,v,w): append (v,w) to list[u]. Time to build: O(E). Space: O(V+E).

connectsTo.reason: This provides the neighbors and weights for relaxation

explanation.codePattern: In Python, use list of lists or dictionaries

examples.trace: Node 0: edge to 1 weight5 → list[0] = [(1,5)]. Node 1: edge to 2 weight3 → list[1] = [(2,3)]. Node 2: no outgoing edges → list[2] = [].
Node 0: edge to 1 weight2 → list[0] = [(1,2)]. Node 1: edge to 0 weight3 → list[1] = [(0,3)].

testCases.input: Graph: 4 nodes (0-3), edges: 0→1 weight=2, 1→2 weight=3, 2→3 weight=4
Graph: 3 nodes (0-2), edges: 0→1 weight=3, 1→2 weight=4, 2→0 weight=5
Graph: 5 nodes (0-4), edges: 0→1 weight=1, 1→2 weight=1, 2→3 weight=1, 3→4 weight=1

approaches.name: Dijkstra with Predecessor List O((V+E) log V)
approaches.summary: Implement Dijkstra's algorithm with a min-heap, but store multiple predecessors in a list per node to handle multiple shortest paths. This allows reconstruction of all shortest paths, not just one.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Dijkstra with Predecessor List for All Shortest Paths
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Dijkstra's algorithm that finds all shortest paths from source to all nodes using predecessor lists.

explanation.prose: This problem requires finding not just the shortest distance from a source node to all other nodes in a weighted graph, but also reconstructing ALL possible shortest paths. Standard Dijkstra's algorithm only tracks one predecessor per node, which gives one shortest path. Here, we modify it to store multiple predecessors in a list for each node whenever we find an equally short path. This allows us to later reconstruct all shortest paths using backtracking. The intuition is that when relaxing an edge (u, v), if dist[u] + weight == dist[v], we've found another equally short path to v, so we add u to v's predecessor list. If it's shorter, we clear the list and add u. We use a min-heap to efficiently select the next node with smallest tentative distance. After distances are computed, we can reconstruct paths by recursively exploring all combinations from target back to source using the predecessor lists.

connectsTo.reason: by using the predecessor list building as the key subroutine

timeComplexity.note: log V) for Dijkstra plus O(P) for path reconstruction where P is total number of paths (could be exponential in worst case)

spaceComplexity.note: for adjacency list and predecessor lists

commonMistakes: not handling multiple equal-distance paths correctly
or having infinite recursion in path reconstruction
explanation.codePattern: Initialize distances to infinity, predecessor lists empty, heap with source

examples.input: Graph: 4 nodes (0-3). Edges: 0-1 weight 1, 0-2 weight 2, 1-3 weight 1, 2-3 weight 1. Source: 0. Target: 3.
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize dist = [0, inf, inf, inf], preds = [[], [], [], []], heap = [(0,0)]. Step 2: Pop (0,0). Relax edges: to node1 (weight1): dist[1]=1, preds[1]=[0], push (1,1). to node2 (weight2): dist[2]=2, preds[2]=[0], push (2,2). Step 3: Pop (1,1). Relax edge to node3 (weight1): dist[3]=2, preds[3]=[1], push (3,2). Step 4: Pop (2,2). Relax edge to node3: dist[3]==2 (equal), so add 2 to preds[3]=[1,2]. Step 5: Pop (3,2). Heap empty. Distances: [0,1,2,2]. Reconstruct paths from node3: preds[3]=[1,2]. From 1: preds[1]=[0] gives path [0,1,3]. From 2: preds[2]=[0] gives path [0,2,3]. All paths: [[0,1,3],[0,2,3]].

examples.input: Graph: 3 nodes (0-2). Edges: 0-1 weight 2, 0-2 weight 3, 1-2 weight 1. Source: 0. Target: 2.
examples.trace: Step 1: dist=[0,inf,inf], preds=[[],[],[]], heap=[(0,0)]. Step 2: Pop (0,0). Relax: to1: dist[1]=2, preds[1]=[0], push (2,1). to2: dist[2]=3, preds[2]=[0], push (3,2). Step 3: Pop (2,1). Relax to2: dist[1]+1=3 == dist[2], so add 1 to preds[2]=[0,1]. Step 4: Pop (3,2). Heap empty. Distances: [0,2,3]. Reconstruct from node2: preds[2]=[0,1]. From 0: direct path [0,2]. From 1: preds[1]=[0] gives [0,1,2]. All paths: [[0,2],[0,1,2]].

testCases.input: Graph: 2 nodes (0,1). Edge: 0-1 weight 5. Source:0 Target:1
Graph: 3 nodes (0,1,2). Edges: 0-1 weight 1, 1-2 weight 1, 0-2 weight 2. Source:0 Target:2
Graph: 4 nodes (0-3). Edges: 0-1 weight 1, 0-2 weight 4, 1-2 weight 2, 1-3 weight 5, 2-3 weight 1. Source:0 Target:3
Graph: 4 nodes (0-3). Edges: 0-1 weight 1, 0-2 weight 1, 1-3 weight 1, 2-3 weight 1. Source:0 Target:3
Graph: 5 nodes (0-4). Edges: 0-1 weight 2, 0-2 weight 3, 1-3 weight 1, 2-3 weight 1, 3-4 weight 1. Source:0 Target:4
Graph: 3 nodes (0,1,2). Edges: 0-1 weight 1, 0-2 weight 1. Source:0 Target:2
Graph: 4 nodes (0-3). Edges: 0-1 weight 1, 1-2 weight 1, 2-3 weight 1, 0-3 weight 3. Source:0 Target:3
Graph: 5 nodes (0-4). Edges: 0-1 weight 1, 1-2 weight 1, 2-3 weight 1, 3-4 weight 1, 0-4 weight 4. Source:0 Target:4
Graph: 3 nodes (0,1,2). Edges: 0-1 weight 2, 1-2 weight 2, 0-2 weight 4. Source:0 Target:2
Graph: 4 nodes (0-3). Edges: 0-1 weight 1, 0-2 weight 2, 1-3 weight 2, 2-3 weight 1. Source:0 Target:3

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Building Predecessor Lists During Dijkstra Relaxation
ladders.desc: Implement the edge relaxation logic that maintains multiple predecessors when equal shortest distances are found.

explanation.prose: This sub-routine is the core modification to standard Dijkstra that enables tracking all shortest paths. During relaxation of an edge from u to v with weight w, we compare the new distance candidate = dist[u] + w with the current dist[v]. If candidate < dist[v], we've found a strictly shorter path: update dist[v] = candidate, clear v's predecessor list (since all previous paths are longer), add u as the only predecessor, and push v to the heap with new distance. If candidate == dist[v], we've found another equally short path: add u to v's predecessor list without clearing it and without pushing v to heap (since distance hasn't changed). If candidate > dist[v], do nothing. The intuition is that predecessor lists act like a reverse adjacency list for shortest paths only. This must be integrated into Dijkstra's main loop. Time complexity per relaxation is O(1) for list operations, so overall O(E) for all relaxations. This feeds into L0 by providing the data needed for path reconstruction.

spaceComplexity.note: worst-case if many equal paths exist
